1: Isaiah 36 begins the 'Historical Appendix' (Isaiah 36-39) that largely parallels 2 Kings 18:13-20:19. This historical narrative provides the concrete crisis that all the preceding oracles (particularly Isaiah 28-35) were responding to: the Assyrian invasion of Sennacherib in 701 BCE. 'Vayehi bearba esreh shanah lamelekh Chizkiyahu, and it was in the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah.' The fourteenth year of Hezekiah = 701 BCE by standard chronology, the third campaign of Sennacherib (705-681 BCE). Sennacherib's own annals (the Taylor Prism in the British Museum) confirm: 'I besieged and conquered 46 fortified cities of Judah... Hezekiah himself I shut up like a caged bird in Jerusalem his royal city.' 'He marched against all the fortified cities of Judah and captured them' — the Lachish letters and archaeological evidence confirm the systematic capture of Judean cities before Jerusalem.
2: 'Then the king of Assyria sent his field commander Rabshakeh from Lachish to King Hezekiah in Jerusalem with a large army' — the Rabshakeh (rāb šāqê = the 'chief cup-bearer' title, but actually a senior diplomatic-military official in the Assyrian court hierarchy, attested in multiple Assyrian documents). From Lachish: Lachish (Tel Lachish, modern Tell ed-Duweir, 30 miles southwest of Jerusalem) was the second most important city of Judah. Sennacherib's siege of Lachish is depicted in extraordinary detail in the Lachish Reliefs (Southwest Palace of Nineveh, now in the British Museum) — 8 carved stone panels showing the siege ramps, battering rams, and the capture and deportation of the population. 'He took up a position at the channel of the upper pool, on the road to the Washerman's Field' — the exact same location as Isaiah 7:3 (where Isaiah met Ahaz during the Syro-Ephraimite crisis of 735 BCE). The topographic connection underscores the parallel: two Assyrian crises, same location, same prophetic word of trust in YHWH.
3: 'Then Eliakim son of Hilkiah, the palace administrator, came out to meet him, along with Shebna the secretary and Joah son of Asaph, the recorder' — the three Judean officials: (1) Eliakim son of Hilkiah (hakkaykim ben-chilqiyahu asher al-habbayit) = the palace administrator/steward, the highest civil office in the royal household. Isaiah 22:20-25 contains YHWH's oracle specifically concerning Eliakim: 'I will call my servant Eliakim son of Hilkiah... I will clothe him with your robe and fasten your sash and hand your authority over to him' — Eliakim is YHWH's appointed replacement for the corrupt Shebna. (2) Shebna the secretary (shebna hassoferyh) — in Isaiah 22:15-19 YHWH had specifically prophesied Shebna's downfall: 'What are you doing here... I am going to hurl you away forcefully, O mighty man, and grasp you firmly.' He has now been demoted from palace administrator to secretary. (3) Joah son of Asaph, the recorder (hamazkirm) = the court historian/chronicler.
4: 'Rabshakeh said to them, Tell Hezekiah: This is what the great king, the king of Assyria, says — What is this confidence you are trusting in?' — the opening of Rabshakeh's rhetorical assault. The diplomatic formula 'this is what the great king, the king of Assyria says' (ko-amar hammelek hagadol melek Ashshur) = the suzerainty treaty opening formula, with Sennacherib's full royal title (hamelekh hagedol = the Great King was the specific Assyrian imperial title used in their royal inscriptions and treaties). The first rhetorical question: 'mah ha'emunah hazot asher batacht, what is this trust which you are trusting?' — batach (trust = the covenant-trust word that runs through the whole oracle-series; Isaiah 30:12, 15; 31:1 have all condemned misplaced trust). Now Rabshakeh uses the same language: what is the basis of Hezekiah's confidence?
5: 'I tell you — mere words are not strategy or battle strength. So whom do you rely on, that you have dared to rebel against me?' — the military-strategic analysis: 'amarti akh-davar sefatayim etsah ugevurah lamilchamah, I say — surely a word of the lips is counsel and strength for battle?' The sarcastic rhetorical question: 'are you counting on a word of the lips (mere talk) for military strategy?' — contrasting diplomatic promises with actual military capability. 'On whom do you rely, that you have rebelled against me?' (al mi batacht ki maradtah bi, on whom have you trusted that you have rebelled against me?) — the political analysis: Hezekiah's refusal to pay tribute (which the Assyrian records call 'rebellion') must have been based on some strategic calculation. Rabshakeh demands: what or who is that calculation?
6: 'Look — you are leaning on Egypt, that cracked and broken reed of a staff. If anyone leans on it, it pierces right through his hand. That is what Pharaoh king of Egypt is to anyone who trusts him' — the Egypt-as-broken-reed assessment: 'hineh batacht al-mishe'en haqaneh hara-tsuts hazzeh, behold you are trusting on the staff of this crushed reed.' The qaneh (reed = the Nile reed used to make walking-sticks and writing pens, plentiful in Egypt; qaneh/kaneh = the reed-staff) that is rasuts (crushed/cracked = structurally compromised, unable to bear weight). Remarkably, Rabshakeh uses the same metaphor for Egypt that Isaiah himself had used in 30:6 and Isaiah 31:1-3! Either (a) Isaiah's prophecies had become widely known, (b) the 'broken reed' was a common ANE proverb for Egyptian unreliability, or (c) the Assyrian military intelligence had absorbed Judean prophetic discourse. 'It pierces right through his hand' (uba bechafpo uvaqavah yaddo, and it enters into his palm and pierces his hand) — the staff that should support becomes a weapon against its user.
7: 'But if you say to me, We trust in the LORD our God, isn't this the very God whose high places and altars Hezekiah removed, telling Judah and Jerusalem, You must worship at this one altar?' — Rabshakeh's deliberate misrepresentation of Hezekiah's reform: 've'im-tomar elai el-YHWH Elohenu batachnu, and if you say to me, In the LORD our God we trust.' Hezekiah's religious reform (2 Kings 18:3-6) had centralized worship in Jerusalem by destroying the high places (bamot) and altars throughout Judah. From an Assyrian perspective (and from those Judeans who valued the local shrines), this looked like Hezekiah had reduced YHWH's worship-sites. Rabshakeh's argument: by removing most of the places where people could worship YHWH, Hezekiah has actually weakened the deity's position. The argument is theologically absurd (YHWH is not diminished by fewer shrines) but rhetorically clever — it exploits the Judeans' folk-theology that more shrines = more divine power.
8: 'Come now — make a wager with my master the king of Assyria: I will give you two thousand horses, if you can actually find riders to put on them' — the military-capability challenge: 'hitarev na et-adoni hammelek Ashshur ve'ettenah lekha alpayim susim, make a wager/exchange with my lord the king of Assyria and I will give you 2,000 horses.' The offer is designed to humiliate: Judah is so lacking in trained cavalry that they couldn't even mount 2,000 horses if given them. This is precisely the concern of Isaiah 30:16-17 (trusting in swift horses) and Isaiah 31:1 (trusting in chariots and cavalry) — Isaiah had warned against Egypt's cavalry, and Rabshakeh now exposes the gap: you don't have cavalry of your own either. Sennacherib's annals describe the Assyrian army's deployment of vast chariot forces — the contrast with Judah's minimal cavalry would have been obvious.
9: 'How then could you hold off even one of the least of my master's junior officers? Yet here you are, trusting Egypt for chariots and horsemen!' — the cascading military impossibility: 've'eikh tashuv et-penei pechath echad avadei adoni haqqetannim, and how will you turn back the face of one prefect of the least of my lord's servants?' The pechath (governor/prefect = a mid-level Assyrian administrative-military officer, not even a general) would be too much for Jerusalem's army. 'Yet here you are, trusting Egypt for chariots and horsemen' (vatibetach lekha al-Mitsrayim lerakhev ulepharashim, and you have trusted to yourself in Egypt for chariots and horsemen) — the Judean strategy exposed as both inadequate (they can't even mount 2,000 horses if given) and misplaced (Egypt couldn't help anyway, as Isaiah 30-31 warned).
10: 'And do you think I have come up against this land without the LORD's approval? The LORD himself said to me: March against this land and destroy it' — the most theologically significant claim in Rabshakeh's speech: 'ha'min bilti YHWH aliythi al-ha'arets hazot leshachitah, was it apart from YHWH that I came up against this land to destroy it?' The claim that YHWH commissioned the Assyrian invasion echoes Isaiah's own oracles: Isaiah 10:5-6 ('Woe to Assyria, the rod of my anger, in whose hand is the club of my wrath; I send him against a godless nation'); 36:10 and Isaiah 10:5-6 are in direct dialogue. Whether Rabshakeh genuinely believed YHWH had spoken (a plausible claim in ANE theological logic where conquered peoples's gods were seen as abandoning them) or was cynically manipulating Judean theology, the claim had prophetic backing that Hezekiah could not simply dismiss.
11: 'Please speak to us your servants in Aramaic, for we understand it. Do not speak Hebrew in the hearing of the people who are on the wall' — the linguistic-diplomatic request: 'dabber-na el-avadekhah Aramit ki shome'iyn anachnu, please speak to your servants in Aramaic for we understand.' Aramaic (Aramit) was the diplomatic lingua franca of the ANE by the 8th century BCE — attested in Assyrian royal inscriptions as the language of international communication (like English today). The Judean officials understood Aramaic (as educated diplomats would). Hebrew (Yehudit = literally 'Judean,' the regional vernacular) was the language of the common people (the soldiers on the wall, the civilian population). The officials' request: conduct the negotiations in diplomatic language to prevent a public-relations disaster. Rabshakeh's refusal (Isaiah 36:12) is deliberate — he wants the population to hear and panic, bypassing the leadership to address the rank-and-file directly.
12: 'Did my master send me to speak these words only to your master and to you? Was he not also sending a message to the men on the wall — who will end up eating their own filth and drinking their own urine alongside you when the siege is over?' — the psychological warfare of direct address to the soldiers: 'ha'al adonekha ve'eleykha shalachani adoni, was it to your lord and to you that my lord sent me?' Rabshakeh deliberately bypasses the diplomatic hierarchy to address the common soldier. The siege-threat: 'le'akhol et-tso'atam velishto et-shineyhem imakem, to eat their dung and drink their urine with you' — the most extreme conditions of starvation-siege: 2 Kings 6:25-29 describes Samaria's siege under Ben-Hadad where donkey's heads sold for 80 shekels and dove's dung for 5 shekels of silver, and even cannibalism occurred. Sennacherib's threat: if you endure the siege, this is what happens. The graphic language is designed to terrify the soldiers into pressuring Hezekiah to surrender.
13: 'Then Rabshakeh stood up and shouted in Hebrew at full volume: Hear the words of the great king, the king of Assyria!' — the public-address pivot: 'vayya'amod Ravshaqeh vayiqra beqol gadol Yehudit, and Rabshakeh stood up and called out in a loud voice in Hebrew/Judean.' Rabshakeh physically stands (the posture of the herald making a public proclamation) and 'calls out' (yiqra = the public herald's proclamation, cf. Isaiah 40:3: 'a voice crying out in the wilderness'). 'In a loud voice in Hebrew' — the diplomatic lingua franca abandoned; Rabshakeh speaks the people's own language. The 'great king' (hammelek hagadol) formula again — Sennacherib's imperial title used in his own inscriptions as hamelekh harabbu (the Great King). The direct appeal to the people over the heads of their leaders is a sophisticated psychological operation.
14: 'This is the king's word: Do not let Hezekiah mislead you — he is not able to deliver you' — the direct undermining of Hezekiah's authority: 'ko-amar hammelek al-yashia lakhem Chizkiyahu, thus says the king: do not let Hezekiah lead you into folly.' Al-yashia (do not let him lead you astray/save you) — yashia from nasha (to lead astray/deceive) = Rabshakeh frames Hezekiah's defiance as deception of his own people. 'He is not able to deliver you' (lo-yukhal lehatssilekhem, he is not able to rescue/deliver you) — the same verb that Rabshakeh will use in Isaiah 36:18-20 for the inability of other gods to deliver. By framing Hezekiah's advocacy for trust in YHWH as deception, Rabshakeh attempts to separate the king from his people's confidence in his leadership.
15: 'And do not let Hezekiah lead you to trust in the LORD by saying, The LORD will certainly deliver us — this city will not fall into the king of Assyria's hands' — Rabshakeh directly attacks the prophetic promise: 'veal-yavtech etkhem Chizkiyahu el-YHWH, and do not let Hezekiah lead you to trust in YHWH.' The target: the specific prophetic promise that Isaiah had been delivering throughout the Assyrian crisis — that YHWH would defend Jerusalem (Isaiah 37:33-35 confirms this promise was given). 'The LORD will certainly deliver us' (hatstsil yatssilenu YHWH, YHWH will surely deliver us) — the double verb (absolute infinitive + finite verb) = emphatic certainty. Hezekiah had been relying on this promise. Rabshakeh's strategy: if he can get the Judeans to doubt the prophetic promise, Hezekiah's resistance collapses without a siege.
16: 'Do not listen to Hezekiah. For this is what the king of Assyria says: Make terms with me and surrender — and every one of you will eat from his own vine and his own fig tree and drink from his own cistern' — the counter-offer of peace: 'al-tishme'u el-Chizkiyahu, do not listen to Hezekiah.' The alternative to Hezekiah's resistance: 'asu itti berakhah, make a blessing/terms with me' (the euphemism for surrender — berakhah = a blessing/gift = the terms of a peaceful capitulation). The promised conditions: 'each man eating from his own vine and fig tree' (ish gepenno ve'ish te'enato, each man his vine and each man his fig tree) = the Solomonic peace-formula of 1 Kings 4:25 ('during Solomon's lifetime Judah and Israel lived in safety, each man under his own vine and fig tree'). Rabshakeh offers to restore the conditions of the golden age — but as Assyrian subjects. 'Drink from his own cistern' (veshatu ish mei-bor, and drink each man water of his cistern) = the domestic water supply undisturbed.
17: 'Until I come and take you to a land just like your own — a land of grain and new wine, a land of bread and vineyards' — the deportation promise dressed as opportunity: 'ad-ba'i velaqachti etkhem, until I come and take you.' The transparent admission: the peace terms include eventual deportation to 'a land just like your own' (el-erets ketartsekhem, a land like your own land) = 'a land of grain and new wine' (erets dagan vetirosh, a land of grain and wine). The offer is essentially the Assyrian resettlement policy: conquered populations were deported to other parts of the empire and replaced with other deportees. Rabshakeh frames this as a positive: the new land is 'just like yours.' This is historically accurate — Assyrian resettlement policy often placed conquered peoples in comparably productive agricultural regions. But the offer is a velvet-gloved deportation threat.
18: 'Watch out — do not let Hezekiah deceive you by saying, The LORD will deliver us. Has any god of the nations ever delivered his land from the power of the king of Assyria?' — the comparative-religion challenge: 'pen-yassit etkhem Chizkiyahu, do not let Hezekiah mislead/entice you.' The final theological argument — and the most powerful: 'hehitsil elohei hagoyim ish arto miyyad melek Ashshur, has any god of the nations delivered his land from the hand of the king of Assyria?' The Assyrian track record: no god of any conquered nation had successfully defended his people against Assyrian conquest. This is historically accurate as Assyrian propaganda — the Assyrian campaign records consistently portray the conquest of cities as the abandonment or defeat of their patron deities. The Assyrian theology: Ashur is greater than all other gods, demonstrated by military conquest. YHWH is just one more local deity who will fail like the rest.
19: 'Where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim? Did they deliver Samaria from my power?' — the list of defeated deities: 'ayeh elohei Chamat ve'Arpad, where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad?' Hamath (modern Hama on the Orontes River in Syria, conquered by Sargon II in 720 BCE). Arpad (modern Tell Rif'at north of Aleppo, conquered by Tiglath-pileser III in 740 BCE and by Sargon II). Sepharvaim (possibly Sibraim between Damascus and Hamath, or the Babylonian Sippar — conquered by Sargon II). Samaria (the capital of the northern Kingdom of Israel, conquered by Sargon II in 722 BCE after a 3-year siege) — the most pointed example: YHWH's own people, the northern kingdom, with their presumably YHWH-related cult, could not resist the Assyrians. The logic: if YHWH didn't save his own people in Samaria, why would he save Jerusalem?
20: 'Which of all the gods of these lands has ever delivered his land from my power? What makes you think the LORD will deliver Jerusalem from me?' — the climactic rhetorical question: 'mi bekhol elohei ha'aratsot ha'elleh asher hitsilu et-artsam miyyadi, who among all the gods of these lands delivered his land from my hand?' The rhetorical force: the cumulative record of divine failure across the region creates an overwhelming case. 'What makes you think the LORD will deliver Jerusalem from me?' (ki-yatsil YHWH et-Yerushalayim miyyadi, that YHWH will deliver Jerusalem from my hand?) — the final pointed application. The argument works on Assyrian theological premises but not on covenantal ones: YHWH is not merely a local patron deity but the creator of all things, the one who uses Assyria itself as his instrument (Isaiah 10:5). Rabshakeh's theology is sophisticated but ultimately ignores Israel's particular covenant claim.
21: 'But they remained silent and said nothing in reply, for the king had commanded, Do not answer him' — the strategic silence: 'vayecherieshu velo-anuhv oto dabar, and they kept silent and did not answer him a word.' The Judean officials' silence is the only appropriate response given the king's command. To answer Rabshakeh's theological challenges would be to engage the enemy's framing on his terms — acknowledging that YHWH's power is in question. The silence is both obedience to Hezekiah's command and a refusal to dignify the comparison of YHWH with the gods of the nations. The theological truth is that YHWH is not comparable to Hamath's gods — but Rabshakeh's framework does not admit this. Silence preserves the distinction. Jesus's silence before Pilate (Matthew 27:14; Mark 15:5; Isaiah 53:7: 'he was led like a sheep to the slaughter... he did not open his mouth') echoes the same dynamic.
22: 'Then Eliakim son of Hilkiah, the palace administrator, Shebna the secretary, and Joah son of Asaph, the recorder, came to Hezekiah with their robes torn and reported to him what Rabshakeh had said' — the report to Hezekiah: the three officials return with their garments torn. 'They came to Hezekiah begihadim qeruyim, with torn garments' — qara (to tear, to rend) is the physical mourning-and-distress gesture (Genesis 37:29: Reuben tore his clothes on finding Joseph gone; 2 Samuel 13:31: David tore his clothes at Amnon's sin; 2 Kings 22:11: Josiah tore his clothes when the Book of the Law was read; Ezra 9:3). The torn robes signal that the Rabshakeh's speech has hit its mark — the officials are in genuine distress. Their report to Hezekiah ('they told him the words of Rabshakeh') sets up Isaiah 37's climax: Hezekiah's prayer, Isaiah's oracle, and YHWH's dramatic deliverance in the night.
